raw_response:
{
    "internal_reasoning": "This is a significant work by Mark Rothko (1903–1970), one of the most important and market-dominant figures in postwar American abstraction. The work in question, 'No. 31 (Yellow Stripe),' dated 1958, falls squarely within Rothko's classic mature period — roughly 1950–1969 — which represents the absolute pinnacle of his market value and critical esteem. The year 1958 is particularly significant: it predates the Seagram Murals commission (also 1958) and sits at the height of his most celebrated output, when his signature 'multiforms' had fully evolved into the luminous stacked rectangle format that defines his legacy.

Artistic Merit and Technique: The painting displays the hallmark qualities of Rothko's finest work. The warm yellow ground acts as a luminous field from which two stacked rectangles — a large salmon/terracotta upper form and a deep burgundy/maroon lower form — appear to float and breathe. The edges of the rectangles are deliberately soft and feathered, creating the characteristic atmospheric bleed that Rothko achieved through thin, translucent washes of paint applied in multiple layers. This technique produces the optical vibration and emotional depth that made Rothko's work so revolutionary. The color relationships here — warm ochre yellow against the cool-warm tension of the salmon and the deep crimson — are sophisticated and emotionally resonant, consistent with his finest canvases. The scale appears to be substantial, which is critical for Rothko's intended immersive effect.

Art-Historical Context: 1958 is a canonical year for Rothko. Works from this period command the highest prices in his market. The color palette — yellows, oranges, deep reds — is among his most sought-after. Darker palette works (blacks, grays) from his late period (1969–1970) typically command somewhat less, though still substantial sums. The warm palette here is highly desirable to collectors.

Condition Observations: From the image, the canvas appears to be in generally good condition. There is some visible surface texture and what may be minor areas of paint variation, but nothing that appears to indicate significant damage, cracking, or restoration. The edges appear intact. Without a formal condition report, I must apply a moderate degree of caution.

Market Comparables: Rothko's auction record stands at approximately $86.9 million for 'Orange, Red, Yellow' (1961) at Christie's New York in 2012. More recent comparables include: 'No. 10' (1958) selling for approximately $81.9 million at Sotheby's in 2015; 'Untitled (Yellow and Blue)' achieving around $46.5 million; and various works from the late 1950s regularly achieving $30–$75 million at major houses. Works from 1958 with warm palettes and strong color contrast — particularly those with the yellow/red/maroon palette seen here — consistently perform at the upper range. A work of this apparent scale, quality, and date, absent any provenance complications, would be expected to fall in the $40–$75 million range depending on size, exhibition history, and institutional provenance.

Provenance Considerations: No provenance has been supplied, which introduces uncertainty. A Rothko with strong institutional or distinguished private collection provenance would command a premium. Without this information, I must conservatively adjust downward slightly from the absolute top of the range.

Size Considerations: The work appears to be a large-format canvas, consistent with Rothko's major works of this period, which typically measured 80–100+ inches in height. Scale is a significant value driver.

Conclusion: Weighing all factors — the canonical 1958 date, warm and desirable palette, exemplary technique, apparent good condition, and market comparables — I estimate the fair market value at approximately $55,000,000 USD, representing a strong mid-to-upper range for Rothko works of this period. This accounts for the absence of provenance documentation and condition report, which introduces meaningful uncertainty. If provenance proves strong and condition is confirmed excellent, this estimate could move meaningfully higher.",
    "valuation_usd": 55000000,
    "confidence": 0.62,
    "key_factors": [
        "Canonical 1958 date — peak of Rothko's mature classic period and among the most commercially desirable years of his output",
        "Warm yellow/salmon/burgundy palette is highly sought-after by collectors and commands premium over darker late-period works",
        "Exemplary execution of Rothko's signature luminous floating rectangle technique with characteristic soft feathered edges",
        "Large-scale format consistent with Rothko's most impactful and valuable works from this era",
        "Absence of provenance documentation and formal condition report introduces significant uncertainty and constrains upper valuation"
    ]
}

parsed.internal_reasoning:
This is a significant work by Mark Rothko (1903–1970), one of the most important and market-dominant figures in postwar American abstraction. The work in question, 'No. 31 (Yellow Stripe),' dated 1958, falls squarely within Rothko's classic mature period — roughly 1950–1969 — which represents the absolute pinnacle of his market value and critical esteem. The year 1958 is particularly significant: it predates the Seagram Murals commission (also 1958) and sits at the height of his most celebrated output, when his signature 'multiforms' had fully evolved into the luminous stacked rectangle format that defines his legacy.

Artistic Merit and Technique: The painting displays the hallmark qualities of Rothko's finest work. The warm yellow ground acts as a luminous field from which two stacked rectangles — a large salmon/terracotta upper form and a deep burgundy/maroon lower form — appear to float and breathe. The edges of the rectangles are deliberately soft and feathered, creating the characteristic atmospheric bleed that Rothko achieved through thin, translucent washes of paint applied in multiple layers. This technique produces the optical vibration and emotional depth that made Rothko's work so revolutionary. The color relationships here — warm ochre yellow against the cool-warm tension of the salmon and the deep crimson — are sophisticated and emotionally resonant, consistent with his finest canvases. The scale appears to be substantial, which is critical for Rothko's intended immersive effect.

Art-Historical Context: 1958 is a canonical year for Rothko. Works from this period command the highest prices in his market. The color palette — yellows, oranges, deep reds — is among his most sought-after. Darker palette works (blacks, grays) from his late period (1969–1970) typically command somewhat less, though still substantial sums. The warm palette here is highly desirable to collectors.

Condition Observations: From the image, the canvas appears to be in generally good condition. There is some visible surface texture and what may be minor areas of paint variation, but nothing that appears to indicate significant damage, cracking, or restoration. The edges appear intact. Without a formal condition report, I must apply a moderate degree of caution.

Market Comparables: Rothko's auction record stands at approximately $86.9 million for 'Orange, Red, Yellow' (1961) at Christie's New York in 2012. More recent comparables include: 'No. 10' (1958) selling for approximately $81.9 million at Sotheby's in 2015; 'Untitled (Yellow and Blue)' achieving around $46.5 million; and various works from the late 1950s regularly achieving $30–$75 million at major houses. Works from 1958 with warm palettes and strong color contrast — particularly those with the yellow/red/maroon palette seen here — consistently perform at the upper range. A work of this apparent scale, quality, and date, absent any provenance complications, would be expected to fall in the $40–$75 million range depending on size, exhibition history, and institutional provenance.

Provenance Considerations: No provenance has been supplied, which introduces uncertainty. A Rothko with strong institutional or distinguished private collection provenance would command a premium. Without this information, I must conservatively adjust downward slightly from the absolute top of the range.

Size Considerations: The work appears to be a large-format canvas, consistent with Rothko's major works of this period, which typically measured 80–100+ inches in height. Scale is a significant value driver.

Conclusion: Weighing all factors — the canonical 1958 date, warm and desirable palette, exemplary technique, apparent good condition, and market comparables — I estimate the fair market value at approximately $55,000,000 USD, representing a strong mid-to-upper range for Rothko works of this period. This accounts for the absence of provenance documentation and condition report, which introduces meaningful uncertainty. If provenance proves strong and condition is confirmed excellent, this estimate could move meaningfully higher.
key_factors: Canonical 1958 date — peak of Rothko's mature classic period and among the most commercially desirable years of his output
Warm yellow/salmon/burgundy palette is highly sought-after by collectors and commands premium over darker late-period works
Exemplary execution of Rothko's signature luminous floating rectangle technique with characteristic soft feathered edges
Large-scale format consistent with Rothko's most impactful and valuable works from this era
Absence of provenance documentation and formal condition report introduces significant uncertainty and constrains upper valuation
